title: Unity-order coupling between free electrons and multiphoton waveguided Fock states
authors: L. Prelat, S. Abdullah, C. I. Velasco, F. J. García de Abajo
abstract: Electron beams enable highly localized near-field excitation of waveguided optical modes, yet their coupling is typically limited by short interaction times along straight-line trajectories with fixed impact parameters. Here, we theoretically demonstrate that electrostatic steering overcomes this limitation by introducing a tunable turning point in grazing electron trajectories, thus controlling the minimum electron--waveguide separation and producing strong coupling to waveguided modes. Specifically, we consider a biased rectangular silicon waveguide, where a repulsive static field deflects a grazing electron. In this configuration, the electron turning point governs both the coupling strength and the modal selectivity, which can be dynamically tuned through the electron incidence angle and the applied bias. In addition, the aloof electron--waveguide interaction suppresses lossy high-energy channels (e.g., above the silicon band gap) while preserving substantial excitation of the targeted waveguided modes. Using a practical biasing configuration and 100~keV electrons, we predict an average yield exceeding ten photons per electron, with voltage-tunable control of the interaction. Our results establish electrostatic steering as a practical route for engineering and enhancing free-electron coupling to waveguided photonic modes.
summary: This paper proposes using electrostatic fields to steer electron beams into grazing trajectories near silicon waveguides. This technique allows for tunable control over the interaction, significantly increasing the number of photons generated per electron while suppressing unwanted energy losses.
main_problem: The coupling between free electron beams and waveguided optical modes is typically limited by short interaction times and a lack of spectral selectivity due to fixed straight-line trajectories.
main_result: Electrostatic steering of electron trajectories creates a tunable turning point that enhances coupling strength, enables modal selectivity, and can produce high photon yields exceeding ten photons per electron.
method: The authors use the Boundary Element Method (BEM) to calculate electrostatic potentials and the local-response approximation to determine momentum-resolved Electron Energy-Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) probabilities.
model_or_system: A system consisting of a 100-200 keV electron beam interacting with a rectangular silicon waveguide, utilizing either vertical or lateral electrostatic gating to deflect the electron trajectory.
key_observables: EELS probability (energy-loss probability), photon yield per electron, and modal selectivity (TE vs. TM modes).
important_parameters: Electron kinetic energy (100-200 keV), impact parameter (b), electron incidence angle, and applied electrostatic bias voltage.
main_assumptions: The silicon waveguide is modeled as a Perfect Electric Conductor (PEC) for DC potential calculations, and the electron velocity is assumed to be nearly constant during the interaction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the electron-waveguide geometry and EELS probability for different impact parameters; Figure 3 illustrates the lateral gating configuration and photon yield vs. incidence angle; Figures 5-7 detail the EELS spectrum, decay of probability with distance, and the electrostatic potential distributions for various gating geometries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of limited electron-waveguide coupling, proposes electrostatic steering as a solution, details the theoretical framework and numerical methods (BEM/EELS), compares different gating configurations (planar vs. lateral), and quantifies the resulting photon yield and tunability.
why_interesting: The work demonstrates a method for generating tunable, high-yield multiphoton Fock states and entangled electron-photon states, which is highly relevant for quantum nanophotonics and quantum light-matter interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Free-electron coupling to a planar waveguide in a parallel-trajectory configuration. (a) Schematic representation of an e-beam moving with velocity v = vˆy at a distance b from a self-standing laterally extended Si waveg- uide of thickness h. (b,c) Wave-vector- and energy-resolved EELS probability per unit of path length [Eq. (1b)] for (b) b = 50 nm and (c) b = 1 nm. (d) Close-up of the low-energy region in (b). Labels indicate the TM and TE nature of the waveguided modes. (e) EELS probability per unit of path length [Eq. (1a)] for different values of b. In all panels, the waveguide thickness is h = 200 nm, and the electron kinetic energy is 200 keV. Solid and dashed green lines in...

FIG. 2. Electron coupling to a planar waveguide for a parabolic trajectory. (a) Schematic representation of an e-beam under grazing incidence on an extended planar Si waveguide of thickness h, separated by a distance d from a top gate biased at a voltage V0. We consider d to be large enough to neglect the electron–waveguide interaction at the point of electron entrance in the capacitor region. The parabolic elec- tron trajectory is determined by the vertical uniform repul- sive electric field EDC = V0/d > 0 and the minimum distance b from the waveguide (turning point). (b) Energy-resolved EELS probability P(ω) [Eq. (6)] for different values of b. (c) Energy-integrated EELS probability N(ωc)...

FIG. 3. Electron coupling to waveguide modes in a biased waveguide geometry. (a) We consider a conductive Si central waveguide (voltage V0) flanked by two Si electrodes (voltage V1) supported on a sapphire substrate. The scheme shows the geometrical parameters of the system (W = 500 nm, W ′ = 250 nm, d = 100 nm, and h = 300 nm in our calculations). We consider 100 keV electrons moving in the y −z plane of specular symmetry with velocity v parallel to the waveguide. The minimum electron–waveguide distance b is determined by the initial incidence angle θ ≪1. (b) EELS probability per unit of path length for a fixed electron–waveguide distance of 50 nm. (c) Relation between incidence angle θ...

FIG. 4. Effect of the image potential model on the threshold electron–waveguide distance. (a) Out-of- plane electron potential energy U(z) = U DC(z) + U im(z) and its DC and image components for EDC = 108 V/m. We in- dicate the turning-point distance b ≈10 nm for a specific normal electron energy E⊥(horizontal dashed line), as de- termined by the intersection with the potential. For b below the threshold value bth, the trajectory impacts the waveg- uide surface at z = 0. We compare the image potential for a PEC and a Si waveguide of 200 nm thickness. (b) Depen- dence of the threshold distance bth on the external DC field EDC, calculated when the image potential is either taken for a PEC...

FIG. 5. Calculation of the EELS probability and its exponential decay away from a 1D waveguide. (a) Transverse cross-section of the geometry under consideration, including an electron (red dot) moving with velocity v at a distance b from the sapphire-supported Si waveguide. (b) EELS probability per unit of path length as obtained from BEM for b = 50 nm, W = 500 nm, h = 300 nm, and 100 keV electrons. (c) Dependence of the peak probability for modes 1 and 2 on b (solid curves) and exponential fits (dashed curves).
